From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

43 administrative subdivisions of Belgium

**Arrondissements of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium")** (plural in [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *arrondissementen*) are [subdivisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") below the [provinces of Belgium](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium"). There are administrative, **judicial and electoral** arrondissements. These may or may not relate to identical geographical areas.

Belgium, a federalized state, geographically consists of three [regions](/wiki/Regions_and_communities_of_Belgium "Regions and communities of Belgium"), of which only [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") are subdivided into five [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") each; [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") is neither a province nor is it part of one.

## Administrative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arrondissements_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Administrative")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Belgische_Bestuurlijke_Arrondissementen.png/330px-Belgische_Bestuurlijke_Arrondissementen.png)](/wiki/File:Belgische_Bestuurlijke_Arrondissementen.png)

Administrative arrondissements of Belgium

The 43 administrative arrondissements are an administrative level between the [municipalities](/wiki/Commune_(subnational_entity) "Commune (subnational entity)") and the provinces. Brussels-Capital forms a single arrondissement for all 19 municipalities in the region by that name.

As an exception, the arrondissement of Verviers has two NUTS codes: BE335 for the French-speaking part and BE336 for the German-speaking part. The latter is identical to the area of the German-speaking community.

## Judicial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arrondissements_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Judicial")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Belgische_Gerechtelijke_Arrondissementen_%282012%29.png/330px-Belgische_Gerechtelijke_Arrondissementen_%282012%29.png)](/wiki/File:Belgische_Gerechtelijke_Arrondissementen_(2012).png)

Judicial arrondissements of Belgium

Belgium has 12 judicial arrondissements:[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

* The arrondissement Liège covers the French-speaking part of the province of Liège
* The arrondissement Eupen covers the German-speaking part of the province of Liège
* The arrondissement Brussels covers the Capital Region and the administrative arrondissement of Halle-Vilvoorde (western part of Flemish Brabant province)
* The arrondissement Leuven covers the administrative arrondissement of Leuven (eastern part of Flemish Brabant province)
* The remaining 8 arrondissements are coterminous with, and have the same names as, the remaining 8 provinces

Until March 31, 2014 Belgium had 27 judicial arrondissements.[[3]](#cite_note-3) These are now sections of today's 12 judicial arrondissements. In addition, the arrondissement Brussels was divided into the sections Brussels and Halle-Vilvoorde.

| Judicial arrondissement | Sections |
| --- | --- |
| [Antwerp](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Antwerp "Arrondissement of Antwerp") | Antwerp, Mechelen, Turnhout |
| [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde "Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde") | Brussels, Halle-Vilvoorde |
| [Eupen](/wiki/Judicial_Arrondissement_of_Eupen "Judicial Arrondissement of Eupen") | Eupen |
| [East Flanders](/wiki/Judicial_Arrondissement_of_East_Flanders "Judicial Arrondissement of East Flanders") | Dendermonde, Ghent, Oudenaarde |
| [Hainaut](/wiki/Judicial_Arrondissement_of_Hainaut "Judicial Arrondissement of Hainaut") | Charleroi, Mons, Tournai |
| [Leuven](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Leuven "Arrondissement of Leuven") | Leuven |
| [Limburg](/wiki/Judicial_Arrondissement_of_Limburg "Judicial Arrondissement of Limburg") | Hasselt, Tongeren |
| [Liège](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Arrondissement of Liège") | Huy, Liège, Verviers |
| [Luxembourg](/wiki/Judicial_Arrondissement_of_Luxembourg "Judicial Arrondissement of Luxembourg") | Arlon, Marche-en-Famenne, Neufchâteau |
| [Namur](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Namur "Arrondissement of Namur") | Dinant, Namur |
| [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Nivelles "Arrondissement of Nivelles") | Nivelles |
| [West Flanders](/wiki/Judicial_Arrondissement_of_West_Flanders "Judicial Arrondissement of West Flanders") | Bruges, Kortrijk, Veurne, Ypres |

## Electoral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arrondissements_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Electoral")]

Until the end of 1999 the [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district") for the election of the parliaments were *electoral arrondissements*; since the start of 2000 these are the ten *[provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium")*. The arrondissement of [Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital") (geographically coinciding with the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region")) is not part of any province and consequently forms its own electoral district.

As the only part of Belgium[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*], the [Parliament of Wallonia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Wallonia "Parliament of Wallonia") still uses electoral arrondissements. Each electoral arrondissement consists of at least one (administrative) arrondissement. There were previously 13 such electoral districts, but they have since been reduced to 11. Each of these electoral districts take their names from the arrondissements they consist of, usually decreasing in order of population.
